coredata.dc:title: A heterostructure BiOCl nanosheets/TiO2 hollow-tubes composite for visible light-driven efficient photodegradation antibiotic
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Sarenqiqige
Liang, Haiou
Li, Chunping
Bai, Jie
coredata.dc:description: A high-efficiency BiOCl/TiO2 heterostructure hollow-tube (BTHTs) with type-II was successfully synthesized by simple electrospinning technology and solvothermal process loaded with BiOCl nanosheets on TiO2 hollow-tubes (THTs). The results of morphology show that, the surface of THTs was covered by flaky BiOCl like pieces of flower. Optical and photo-electrochemical characterizations show that the composite catalyst has visible light absorption and high photo-generated electron-hole separation ability. Comparing with THTs and powder BiOCl, the BTHTs composites exhibited highest adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity in the photodegradation of Tetracycline (TC), in visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm). The composites could also degrade Chlortetracycline, Demeclocycline, Doxycycline and Oxytetracycline efficaciously. Additionally, trapping experiments were carried out, and the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibiotic photodegradation
Heterostructure
BiOCl/TiO2hollow-tubes
Visible-light photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of prepared TiO2 @ PAN (a), THTs (b) and BTHTs (c); TEM, HRTEM images (d and e), the element mapping of Ti, O and Bi (f, h, i, and j), EDX spectrum (g) of BTHTs.
XRD patterns of the THTs, BiOCl and BTHTs composite (a), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution plots of THTs (b) and BTHTs composite (c).
High resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p (a), Bi 4f (b), Cl 2p (c), and O 1s (d).

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), optical band gap energy Eg (b), transient photocurrent response (c), Nyquist plots of the electrochemical impedance spectra (d) and The PL spectrum (e) for THTs, BiOCl and BTHTs.

Photocatalytic degradation curves (a), pseudo first-order kinetic fitting curves (b) and the corresponding apparent rate constants, photocatalytic degradation of other antibiotics with BTHTs sample (c).
Cycling runs for the TC photocatalytic degradation over BTHTs photocatalyst (a), XRD patterns of fresh and after photocatalytic (b).
Degradation curves of TC with additions of scavengers over BTHTs under visible light.
Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanism over the BiOCl/THTs heterostructure composite.
The comparison of photocatalytic performance of different materials for TC.

head.ce:title: A heterostructure BiOCl nanosheets/TiO2 hollow-tubes composite for visible light-driven efficient photodegradation antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering College, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot, 010051, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering College, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot, 010051, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Industrial Catalysis, Hohhot, 010051, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Industrial Catalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inner Mongolia Key Laboratory of Industrial Catalysis, Hohhot, 010051, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Chemical Engineering College, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot, 010051, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 exhibited heterojunction one-dimensional composite photocatalytic material, with a morphology that bismuth oxychloride nanosheets growing vertically and uniformly on the surface of titanium oxide hollow-tubes. Which was applied in photodegradation of tetracycline, the photocatalytic performance and photocatalytic mechanism of this materials were carried out.

A high-efficiency BiOCl/TiO2 heterostructure hollow-tube (BTHTs) with type-II was successfully synthesized by simple electrospinning technology and solvothermal process loaded with BiOCl nanosheets on TiO2 hollow-tubes (THTs). The results of morphology show that, the surface of THTs was covered by flaky BiOCl like pieces of flower. Optical and photo-electrochemical characterizations show that the composite catalyst has visible light absorption and high photo-generated electron-hole separation ability. Comparing with THTs and powder BiOCl, the BTHTs composites exhibited highest adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity in the photodegradation of Tetracycline (TC), in visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm). The composites could also degrade Chlortetracycline, Demeclocycline, Doxycycline and Oxytetracycline efficaciously. Additionally, trapping experiments were carried out, and the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: The most widespread use antibiotics for the treatment of bacterial infections around the world, tetracycline (TC), abuse of it caused serious pollution of the water environment [1,2]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to find for eliminate antibiotics in water, but it is still a significant challenge to rationally design purification techniques [3,4]. In recent years, researchers have found that the most effective way to eliminate organic pollutants in water is photocatalytic degradation which is regarding as an advanced green and sustainable technology [5–10]. Among the variety of semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) was widely employed owing to its low-cost, non-toxicity, and high-stability and resistant to corrosion in the process of photodegradation [11–16]. However, electron-conductive n-type TiO2 with a wide band gap (anatase ∼ 3.2 eV), low utilization of visible light, a short lifetime of photogenerated carriers, their quantum efficiency still needs to be further improved [25,36]. To overcome this issue, find a way to both narrow the wide band gap and restrain the photo-induced electron-hole pair recombination is critical. Therefore, introducing other oxide materials to form heterojunction with titanium oxide is the most common choice, because of strong red-ox capabilities, could create a built inner electric field around the interface and effectively separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs [26,27]. Among various bismuth-based photocatalysts such as BiVO4 [28], Bi2WO6 [29], and Bi2MoO6 [30], bismuth oxyhalide BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) material has a unique layered structure consists of a layer of [Bi2O2]2+ slabs interleaved by double slabs of [X]−, finally forming a stack of [-X-Bi-O-O-Bi-X-] on one another by nonbonding van der Waals interactions [31–33]. In particular BiOCl, with a band gap of 3.4 eV is a hole-conductive p-type semiconductor with a higher redox potential than TiO2, can formed p-n junction heterostructure with TiO2, and could prolongs the lifetime of photo-generated carriers, to helps improve its photocatalytic activity have been reported [34–37]. So far, Wang et al [38] reported that fabricated a BiOCl/TiO2 heterojunction by a solvothermal method was exhibited great photodegradation activity for 150 ppm gaseous benzene with excellent repeat stability. A composite material of TiO2-BiOCl-ZnCr-Ex was synthesized by Liu et al. [39] that were displayed favorable photodegradation activity for RhB in visible-light, which was attributed to the synergetic effect formed by TiO2 and BiOCl, which enhanced the light absorption and separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Song et al. [36] reported that the hetero-interface between BiOCl and TiO2 provides extra sodium storage sites and a built-in electric field accelerates electron and ion diffusion. Concisely, BiOCl-TiO2 heterostructure materials have been extensively researched in the fields of photocatalytic degradation of harmful gases and liquids, and energy storage. Therefore, many kinds of supports have been used for dispersing nanoparticles. Compared with the others structures, more attention has been paid to one-dimensional (1D) TiO2 tubes, obtained by electrospinning technology [17–19], due to their excellent electron transport capacity, well light scattering ability and high mechanical strength [18–24].

Herein, a heterojunction material from BiOCl loaded on the one-dimensional TiO2 hollow-tubes (BiOCl/TiO2 HTs) was synthesized by simply process that combine of electrospinning, solvothermal and calcine. The catalytic performance of the obtained composites was evaluated through the photodegraded experiments that the same class of antibiotics (such as tetracycline, hlortetracycline, demeclocycline, doxycycline and oxytetracycline) in visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). The composite showed superior photocatalytic activity due to the excellent adsorption performance and high efficiency of electron-holes pair separation. Additionally, trapping experiments were carried out, the formed O2 − and h+ radicals accelerated the photodegradation of tetracycline molecules. Possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, Mw = 80,000) was provided by Kunshan Hongyu Plastics Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %) was supplied by Beijing Chemical Co. (China). Tetrabutyl titanate (TBT) and 1.4-Benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagent Co. (China). Ethylene glycol (EG), Ammonium oxalate (AO) and Iso- propyl alcohol (IPA) were provided by Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. Glacial acetic acid (GAA) was supplied by Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Absolute ethyl alcohol (AEA) and Sodium azide (NaN3) were purchased from Fengchuan (Tianjin) Chemical reagent thecnologies Co., Ltd. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) was provided by Xiya (Shandong) Chemical Co., Ltd. Potassium chloride (KCl) was supplied by Tianhe (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetracycline (TC) was purchased from Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Oxytetracycline and Chlortetracycline were provided by Xiya (Shandong) chemical industry co., Ltd. Doxycycline was supplied by Solarbio (Beijing) technology Co., Ltd. Demeclocycline were purchased from MedChemExpress (USA). All chemicals were directly used without any further purification.

Firstly, PAN powder (1 g) was dissolved in DMF (9 g) at room temperature under vigorous stirring for 24 h, to obtain a homogeneous solution for electrospinning. The solution was transferred into a spinning tube, a copper ring was on the front of the tube to connect to the positive electrode, and an electrostatic spinning process was performed at a direct voltage of 16 KV and a spinning distance of 20 cm. The film of PAN nanofibers was collected on an aluminum plate connected to the negative electrode. Afterwards, titania hollow-tubes (TiO2 HTs) were prepared by sol-gel method, solvothermal method and calcination method. Briefly, under magnetic stirring conditions, GAA (0.15 mL) and TBT (5 mL) were sequentially added to AEA (30 mL) to obtain a uniformly dispersed titanium source precursor solution, a PAN film (0.15 g) was added to the solution, placed for 24 h physical adsorption. The resulting suspension was transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, with solvothermal process heated at 180 °C for 9 h and then naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained yellow fiber membrane (TiO2 @ PAN) was washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol and filtered several times, and air-dried in an oven at 60 °C for 8 h. In order to remove the PAN, the sample was calcined in ambient air at 450 °C for 2 h, titanium oxide hollow-tubes (marked THTs) were obtained. Finally, bismuth oxychloride nanosheets were loaded on the titanium oxide hollow-tubes by a solvothermal method. Specifically, dissolve Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.09308 g) and KCl (0.0143 g) in EG (10 mL) and AEA (5 mL) mixed solvents and stir for 1 h. After mixing the two solutions, continue to stir for 20 min. A solvothermal reaction was performed at 180 °C for 9 h, and the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained powder was washed with deionized water and AEA, centrifuged several times, then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 8 h. Finally, a bismuth oxychloride nanosheets supported titanium oxide hollow-tubes heterojunction nanostructures (BiOCl/ TiO2 HTs, marked BTHTs) was obtained.
The preparation of BiOCl powder was the same as the above preparation conditions without adding TNTs.

The morphology of the samples were observed on a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) instrument (Phenom Pro, Holland), the mapping and EDX patterns was collected on another field emission SEM instrument (FE-SEM, Hitachi Su8220, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) test was carried out on a JEM-2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The phase characterization of the samples were measured on an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD Smart-lab, Japan) instrument with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.154 nm), and scanning rate of 10°/min in the 2θ ranges from 10 to 80°. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area were measured by nitrogen adsorption-desorption method using a Quanta chrome ASIQM 0001-3 instrument (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi (USA) spectrometer with Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Ultraviolet visible absorption spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis 2700 spectrophotometer (Japan). Reflectance measurements were performed on powdered samples, BaSO4 was used as a standard reference. The PL spectra were acquired by an Edinburgh Analytical Instrument (FL-FSTCSPC920 Spectrophotometer, England).

The photo-electrochemical examination of the as-prepared catalyst was measured by a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) with three-electrode cell, where Pt electrode as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. working electrode was made of the sample (5 mg) was added into 10 % Nafion ethanol solution (100 μL) to get slurry, and then coated onto a Indium tin oxide (ITO) glass (2 × 2 cm2), and dried at 80 °C for 4 h. Supporting electrolyte was employed by Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M) in the experiments. The transient photocurrent response (PC) was measured on an applied voltage of 0.3 V with the light on or off every 20 s. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was collected on an applied voltage of 0.3 V with an amplitude of 0.05 V over a frequency between 100000−0.01 Hz. The above-mentioned experiments were used 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter acting as light source.

The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples were evaluated by the photodegradation of antibiotics (TC, Chlortetracycline, Demeclocycline, Doxycycline and Oxytetracycline), used of a light source that 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, CeAulight) with a 420 nm cut-off filter. Experiments were carried out at 25 ± 2 °C by a circulating water system to maintain the reaction isothermally. Typically, the sample (50 mg) was dispersed into reaction solution (100 mL) of antibiotics (20 mg/L). Before irradiation, the system was stirred in the darkness for 30 min to reach the adsorption/ desorption equilibrium. There are about 3 mL solution was taken out at the different irradiation time, filtered (Millipore filter 0.22 μm) and tested by the Shimadzu UV–vis 2700 spectrophotometer (Japan), via the intensity changes of the absorption peak at 357, 367, 366, 345 and 353 nm, to determine the concentration of TC, Chlortetracycline, Demeclocycline, Doxycycline and Oxytetracycline at different time. For recycling experiment, the used catalyst was collected by filtrated washed, dried and then was used for another run to test the stability of the composite.

Radical trapping experiments were performed to identify the active species produced during the photocatalytic degradation of TC by BTHTs composites. There are AO (0.2 mmol·L−1), IPA (2 mmol·L−1), NaN3 (0.2 mmol·L−1) and BQ (0.2 mmol·L−1) were employed as the scavengers for photo-induced holes (h+), the hydroxyl radical (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and the superoxide radical (·O2-), respectively. At the beginning of the reaction, scavengers were dissolved in the TC solution, and then carried out degradation experiments one by one in the same operation as above.

The morphology of the catalytic material has a great influence on the photocatalytic performance. According to the SEM images result (see Fig. S1), the as- electrospinned membranes consist of PAN nanofibers with a diameter of about 200 nm, and the surface is clean and smooth. Pure BiOCl powder show that the spherical structure of the porous surface, which is composed of the dense packing of BiOCl nanosheets, which indicating that it is caused by Ostwald ripening. The red circle marks on the Fig. 1 a image shows a clear coating and fracture. This can be attributed to the covering of the outer layer of TiO2 along the soft PAN fiber structure by solvothermal method. After calcined in air, the PAN fibers were removed and the outer TiO2 tubular structure was left. The diameter of the tube is equivalent to the thickness of the PAN fiber, and the thickness of the tube wall is also about 200 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. Fig. 1c shows SEM image of the resulting BTHTs, BiOCl nanosheets grow vertically and randomly on the surface of TiO2 hollow-tubes, after the solvothermal process, in which the BiOCl nanosheets with 400 nm diameters. That clearly shows the perfect shape of the nanosheets wrapped hollow tube, with uniform size and a petal-shaped surface. The TEM image (Fig. 1d) of the BTHTs further verifies the flower-like BiOCl nanosheets grown on the tubular shape of TiO2. This is consistent with the observation from SEM. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to reveals the heterojunction formation of TiO2 and BiOCl crystals with well-defined lattice fringes. As shown in Fig. 1e, Two kinds of stripes inter-planar distances of 0.344 and 0.352 nm were observed, corresponding respectively to the planes of BiOCl (101) and TiO2 (101). Additionally, Fig. 1(f–j) shows the element mapping and EDX spectrum of BTHTs, it is indicated that the presence of Ti, O, Bi and Cl elements, which are uniformly distributed across the 1D tubular sample. These characteristics fully illustrate the morphology of bismuth oxychloride nanosheets growing vertically and tightly on the surface of titanium oxide tubes in BiOCl/TiO2 HTs composites.

To further determine the structure of crystal and pore for the prepared samples, XRD and BET were used to characterize them. Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of THTs, BiOCl and BTHTs. The positions and relative intensities of the diffraction peaks of the XRD patterns of BTHTs at 2θ = 25.36, 37.87, 48.21, 54.20 and 55.27° are corresponding to (101), (004), (200), (105) and (211) crystal planes of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS 21-1272), respectively [36]. Simultaneously, the key peaks appearing in 2θ = 11.8, 25.91, 32.63, 33.48 and 46.73°, which are characteristic of the (001), (101), (110), (102) and (200) crystal facets of tetragonal structure BiOCl (JCPDS 06-0249) [42]. The composite photocatalyst spectra shows that the peaks of TiO2 and BiOCl correspond accurately to the characteristic diffraction peaks of the standard card, and no impurity peak is observed, confirming the high purity of the two metal oxides. This result is consistent with the phenomena observed by SEM, TEM and HRTEM.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, BJH pore size distribution curves (inset) and specific surface area (SBET) of THTs and BTHTs were displayed in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. Both isotherms are identified as type IV adsorption branch with a narrow hysteresis loop of type H3, which is characteristic of mesoporous materials. The pore-size distribution obtained from the isotherm indicates a number of pores in the sample. It could be noticed that the pore size distribution of the BTHTs sample is wider than that of THTs, while the SBET was smaller than that of THTs, which might be attributed to the formation of the heterogeneous structure. The presence of mesoporous with different diameters can contribute to the adsorption and dispersion of contaminant molecules in the process of wastewater treatment, to enhancing the catalytic activity of the material [40].

Surface analysis of the heterojunction composite was carried out using XPS (see Fig. S2). The survey scan XPS spectra, which clearly demonstrated that the BTHTs samples were mainly composed of Bi, Ti, Cl, and O, elements, which is consistent with the result of element mapping (Fig. 1). Portion (a) and (b) of Fig. 3 are the spectra of the Ti 2p and Bi 4f elements compared THTs and BiOCl with BTHTs heterojunction composite catalyst, respectively. In Fig. 3a, two peaks at 458.5 and 464.2 eV in BTHTs belong to the Ti 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, which confirms Ti4+ ions in TiO2 [41]. In addition, a broad bump appears in the vicinity of 466.7 eV, which could be ascribed to the characteristic the peaks of Ti 2p1/2 and Bi 4d3/2 are partially overlapped [42]. However, there are a small peak appears at the binding energy of 460.4 eV, which can be indexed to higher charge Ti ions [38]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the splitting peaks of Bi 4f centered at 159.1 and 164.4 eV are related to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, revealing that bismuth in BiOCl exists mainly in the chemical state of Bi3+ [31]. Fig. 3c displays the XPS spectra of Cl 2p, where the binding energies located at 197.8 and 199.3 eV could be ascribed to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, confirming the existence of Cl− in BiOCl. The O 1s spectrum was be convoluted into three peaks at 529.7, 531.9 and 533.3 eV (in Fig. 3d). The peak at 529.7 eV is attributed to the Bi-O bond in BiOCl, while the peak at 531.9 eV is assigned to the Ti-O bond of TiO2. The peak at 533.3 eV belongs to surface hydroxyl groups, which can capture holes to generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [31].

It must also be mentioned that the peaks of Ti 2p in BTHTs shifts toward higher binding energy by approximately 0.15 eV in comparison with that of THTs. In contrast, the binding energy of Bi 4f peak of BTHTs shifts to lower than that of pure BiOCl, about 0.48 eV. The reverse shift of Ti 2p and Bi 4f is the definition of Ti-O-Bi chemical bonds formed, which is strong evidence to the construction of heterojunction between TiO2 and BiOCl [43]. In addition, the movement direction of the binding energy can be used to determine the gain or loss of the electrons. Therefore, the electrons are transferred from TiO2 to BiOCl in the composite [44]. Based on the above discussion, it is shown that the combination of TiO2 and BiOCl forms a strong chemical bond, and a heterojunction is formed at their interface, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus for enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

After clarifying the surface interaction behavior of the BiOCl/TiO2 heterojunction hollow-tubes, the light absorptivity and interfacial charge transfer is also crucial for the degradation photocatalysis. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis DRS of THTs, BiOCl, and BTHTs, to further investigate the visible-light response of that. It can be seen that THTs and BiOCl possess strong absorption at ultraviolet region, have yet weak absorption in the visible region. Besides, the optical absorption edge of BTHTs displays red shift obviously in comparison with THTs and BiOCl, and have visible-light response [31,42–44].

According to the Kubelka-Munk formula (1), Fig. 4a is converted into the Fig. 4b, and the band gap E g of the samples was estimated [45].
(1) (αhv)n = A(hv – E g )
Where α, h, ν and A is absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency and proportionality constant, respectively. The value of n depends on the transition in the semiconductor, n = 1/2 for indirect band gap semiconductors and n = 2 for direct. BiOCl belongs to the former [44]. The abscissa axis of the Fig. 4b is hv (conversion method: 1240/λ), and the vertical ordinate is (αhν)1/2. Fit the linear section of the curve in Fig. 4b to estimate its intercept on the X axis, measured the E g value. The band gap of THTs and BiOCl are 3.17 and 3.39 eV. Clearly, BTHT has the smallest band gap (2.94 eV), which could be attributed to the heterojunction formed by the TiO2 hollow tube and BiOCl nanosheets, that significantly improves the capture of visible light and plays a key role in enhances photocatalysis activity in visible light. Transient photocurrent (PC) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are the basic methods for characterizing the interfacial charge transfer properties of semiconductor composites [46,47]. Fig. 4c shows that the THTs and BiOCl photo-electrodes only generated a negligible photocurrent, while the BTHTs heterojunction photo-electrodes generated larger photocurrent at the moment of visible light irradiation. After maintaining for 20 s, the current rapidly decays to a lower level when the light was turned off; this behavior is to be expected and will be further discussed below. The situation of charge transfer resistance at the photo-electrode interface was determined by the arc radius on the Nyquist spectrum, smaller arc radius reflects higher charge transfer efficiency, and the opposite indicates that was lower. As shown in Fig. 4d, BTHT has the smallest arc radius, which is consistent with PC test results. Fig. 4e displays the PL emission spectrum of THTs, BiOCl and BTHTs with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. As present in the figure, the peak of pure BiOCl with high intensity ranges from 420 to 436 nm. The PL spectrum of BTHTs composite shows a similar trend to that of pure THTs, while the intensity of the former is slightly lower. As it’s well known, the intensity of PL emission spectrum correlates with the recombination rate of the charge carriers, and a lower intensity implies a lower electron-holes pair recombination rate [48,49]. These means that the heterojunction structure formed by the special tubular TiO2 and BiOCl nanosheets can transfer the interface charge faster and lead to the effective separation of electron-hole pairs, which will effectively improve the photocatalytic activity.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of composites, the degradation of TC was using THTs, BiOCl, and BTHTs were tested under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). All samples were reached adsorption-desorption equilibrium within 30 min dark reaction, and showed different adsorption capacity for TC. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the blank experiment was carried out without any photocatalyst, and the self-degraded of TC is nearly negligible. The photocatalytic degradation rate of TC using THT has a removal only 22.49 %, and the degradation rate of pure BiOCl is 65.14 %. Among those samples, BTHTs heterostructure composite showed the highest adsorption performance (28.27 %) and photocatalytic efficiency (84.04 %), in 120 min irradiation time, which could be attributed to the growth of BiOCl nanosheets on the surface of TiO2 hollow-tubes to build heterojunction, thereby enhancing the visible light absorption capacity and the separation rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, increasing the photocatalytic degradation activity. To further comprehend the obtained composite photocatalysts for degradation properties of TC, it is necessary to study its kinetic behavior. The kinetic curve accords well with the pseudo-first-order model: ln (C 0/ C) = kt + b, where C 0 (mg·L−1) is the initial concentration and C (mg·L−1) is the residual concentration at the irradiation time t, b is the intercept constant and apparent rate constant k calculated by the model [50]. The k values for different samples are shown in Fig. 5b. Obviously, BTHTs (0.0137 min−1) was about 6.23 times higher than that of THTs (0.0022 min−1), and 2.04 times than that of pure BiOCl (0.0067 min−1).

Other broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotics (such as Chlortetracycline, Dimecycline, Doxycycline, and Oxytetracycline) were also selected for photodegradation to better understand the universality of BTHT photocatalysts. Initial concentration of those solutions were all 20 mg L−1, as shown in Fig. 5c, 75.06 % Chlortetracycline, 72.16 % Demeclocycline, 70.74 % Doxycycline and 50.94 % Oxytetracycline could be degraded, respectively. Further certified that the universality of as-prepared BTHTs catalysts for photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic in visible light irradiation and, worth to application in practical degradation.

Moreover, Table 1 summarizes the photocatalytic degradation activities of different photocatalysts for TC reported in recent years. Comparing with the reported photocatalyst, the BTHTs material prepared in this work has excellent degradation activity for TC in visible light irradiation with longer wavelength, shorter reaction time. Therefore, the results further prove that the TiO2 empty tube composite photocatalyst combined with BiOCl nanosheets can be used as a promising photocatalyst for TC degradation.

In practical applications, the stability of photocatalysts is also important. Thus, the recyclability of BTHTs was evaluated under the same conditions using a five-cycle degradation process. At the end of each cycle, the catalyst was separated, washed and dried for reused in the next cycle. As shown in Fig. 6 a, there is considerable decrease in adsorption performance of 20 %, after the second use, However the TC photocatalytic degradation ratio could reach 72.7 %, and still remained at 86.5 % of the initial degradation efficiency. Interestingly, in the next three cycle experiments, it also showed the same degradation process as the second one. In addition, XRD characterization was used to analyze the changes of the BTHTs sample after five photocatalytic reactions and that of before the reaction (Fig. 6b), and it was found that the phase and structure of the composites had almost no obvious discrepancy. These results indicate that the BiOCl/TiO2 heterostructure of the prepared BTHTs photocatalyst is sufficiently stable and reusable, and can be considered as a qualified candidate for photodegradable antibiotic in practical applications.

The existence of tubular shape and heterojunction structures in BTHTs composites can generation of more free radical species with strong oxidation ability. To unfold the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of TC on the BiOCl/THTs heterojunction composite, trapping experiments were performed; several kinds of scavengers were used to confirm the different active species. As shown in the Fig. 7 , IPA and NaN3 had almost no effect on TC degradation, indicating that ·OH, and 1O2 have no influence on this reaction. However, AO has a little inhibitory effect, indicating that h+ has a certain influence. Surprisingly, when the BQ was added, the inhibitory effect on TC photodegradation was very significant at the moment the light was turned on, and until the reaction was over, the degradation rate of TC was almost remain the adsorption behavior (22 %). The results showed that the ·O2 − contributed the most to the TC photocatalytic degradation of BTHTs.

Based on the above characterization and test results, the possible mechanism of BTHTs composite for photocatalytic degradation of TC was proposed. The band location of two metal oxides can be determined by the following formulas [53]:
(1)
E
V B
= X −
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(2)
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E g
Where E VB and E CB represent the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), respectively. E e is energy of electron versus hydrogen (4.5 eV), X is the absolute electronegativity (values of TiO2 and BiOCl are 5.81 and 6.34 eV) [2], and their band gap E g value is 3.17 and 3.39 eV (as shown in Fig. 4) [44]. Hence, the calculated CB and VB of TiO2 are -0.275 and 2.895 V vs. NHE, respectively, while the CB and VB of BiOCl are 0.145 and 3.535 V, respectively, vs. NHE [54,55]. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the BiOCl/TiO2 HTs sample was excited by visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) owing to its narrower band gap (i.e. 2.94 eV). Both BiOCl and TiO2 produced photogenerated electron-hole pairs (e− and h+). The formation of an inner electric field at the interface due to the type-II heterojunction constructed by TiO2 and BiOCl, the CB of TiO2 is negative than that of BiOCl, so the electrons could transfer from the CB of TiO2 to that of BiOCl, this is supported by the analysis of XPS. Meanwhile, h+ transferred to the VB of TiO2 from that of BiOCl. Therefore, the photo-generated electron-hole pairs could be separated effectively, which can also be consistent with the observation from the PC and EIS analysis results (Fig. 4c and d). For the TC reaction system, the photo-generated electrons in the CB of BiOCl could reacted with dissolved O2 in water to generated ·O2 − active radicals [56], and this could attack to the molecules of TC and gradually degrade into CO2 and H2O. Additionally, the h+ in VB of TiO2 could directly oxidize TC molecules to CO2 and H2O due to their strong oxidation ability.

In summary, a visible light driven (λ ≥ 420 nm) heterostructure material (BTHTs) from BiOCl loaded on the one-dimensional TiO2 hollow-tubes (THTs) was synthesized by simply process that combine of electrospinning, solvothermal and calcine. The results of morphology show that, the surface of TiO2 hollow-tubes were covered by flaky BiOCl like pieces of flower. Optical and photoelectrochemical characterization show that the composite catalyst has visible light absorption and high photo-generated electron-hole separation ability. Comparing with THTs and powder BiOCl, BTHTs composite showed the highest adsorption performance (28.27 %) and photocatalytic efficiency (84.04 %), in 120 min irradiation time. And the kinetic behavior accords with the pseudo-first-order model, and the value of apparent rate constant was 0.0137 min−1. There are still remained at 86.5 % of the initial degradation efficiency after five cycles. The as-prepared composites could also photodegraded other tetracycline antibiotics (Chlortetracycline, Demeclocycline, Doxycycline and Oxytetracycline) efficaciously. Trapping experiments show that
O2- and h+ plays the major role in TC photodegradation process, and the possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed. Therefore, it can provide reference for the design of hollow-tubes morphologies, heterojunction structure efficient photocatalysts and to be applied in organic pollution treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the link project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21766022), Science research and innovation Program of Postgraduates of Inner Mongolia autonomous region (B20191129Z).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112590.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
